nativeRange.habitat: Steep outer reef slopes between roughly 7 and 100 m (typically 20–70 m), where individuals shelter in ledges, caves and crevices and rarely venture more than a few centimetres from an escape hole.

diet.notes: In the wild, has been reported to graze on encrusting growth, sponges, tunicates and algal films among reef rubble. In captivity, generally advised to offer multiple small feedings per day of varied marine fare — Spirulina, marine algae sheets, high-quality angelfish preparations (ideally including sponge-based formulas), mysis and other small meaty items. A mature system with grazable live rock tends to help reluctant specimens transition; sponge inclusion in prepared foods is commonly considered important for long-term success.

avoidWith: boisterous tangs or large angels that bully shy fish

summary: The Multibar Angelfish is a deep-reef dwarf angel from the Indo-Pacific, recognized by its distinctive pattern of alternating dark and pale vertical bars. Captive care is considered expert-level, and feeding is the primary challenge — wild-caught specimens tend to refuse prepared foods and are at high risk of starvation without careful diet planning.
careNotes: The Multibar Angelfish is widely classified as expert-only, and wild-caught specimens are rarely offered in the trade because they adapt poorly to prepared foods and have a high mortality rate in captivity. Captive-bred specimens from specialty aquaculture producers are now available through several online retailers and tend to acclimate much more readily. Wild specimens are typically collected from steep outer reef slopes at 20–70 m where they shelter in caves and crevices, so a mature reef system with abundant live rock, deep caves and shaded overhangs is generally advised — replicating that structural complexity helps reduce stress on shy specimens. Subdued lighting, or at least a deeply shaded section of the rockwork, is commonly considered helpful at first, with gradual brightening over time as the fish settles in. Reef compatibility is rated with caution: isolated cases of nipping at sessile invertebrates, LPS coral polyps (large-polyp stony corals), soft corals and clam mantles (Tridacna clams in particular) have been reported, though captive-bred specimens are reported to be less destructive. Tankmate selection matters — keeping only calm, similarly-sized or smaller fish that will not outcompete it at feeding time is generally advised, and adding more than one Multibar (or another dwarf angel) to the same system is not recommended unless introduced as a carefully matched pair. Feeding is the major captive hurdle: the species is widely noted to be a finicky feeder that commonly refuses prepared foods, and wild-caught specimens in particular are at high risk of starvation if they are not eating within the first week. Multiple small feedings per day, sponge-containing prepared foods, and a mature live-rock system with grazable surfaces tend to give the best results. The species is generally considered sensitive to transport and acclimation stress. Despite these husbandry challenges, the Multibar Angelfish is listed as Least Concern on the IUCN Red List.
breedingNotes: The Multibar Angelfish is a pelagic broadcast spawner, releasing eggs and sperm into the water column where fertilization occurs. Captive rearing has reportedly been achieved at a commercial scale, and captive-bred specimens are now available from several specialty aquaculture producers and online retailers — though breeding at the hobbyist level is not routine. Like most dwarf angelfish, individuals are generally considered protogynous hermaphrodites, meaning females are capable of becoming male as social circumstances require. They tend to form loose harems on the reef, with one dominant male presiding over several females; the largest or most dominant fish in a group tends to be the one that undergoes the sex change.

primary.name: LiveAquaria
primary.notes: Source of aquarium husbandry parameters (tank size 70 gal / ~265 L, water parameters, semi-aggressive temperament, with-caution reef rating, finicky-feeder warning, omnivorous diet, expert-only care level).

additional.name: FishBase
additional.notes: Cross-checked taxonomy (family Pomacanthidae, order Acanthuriformes, original description Smith & Radcliffe, 1911), max size (12 cm TL — broadly agrees with LiveAquaria's 4.5 in / ~11 cm), depth range (7–100 m, usually 20–70 m), preferred temperature (25.3–29.1 °C, mean 27.5 °C), Indo-Pacific distribution (Cocos-Keeling Atoll to Society Islands, north to Yaeyama Islands, south to Great Barrier Reef), reclusive biology, and IUCN Least Concern status. FishBase flags that specimens 'usually starve when kept in captivity' — a key driver of the difficult feedingDifficulty rating.

additional.name: ReefApp
additional.notes: Used as a second secondary after Reef2Reef wiki returned no match and Reefs.com returned HTTP 403. Cross-checked minimum tank size (300 L), reef-safe rating (often reef safe with caveats — clam-nipping risk), diet emphasis on Spirulina/sponges/small crustaceans, protogynous hermaphrodite reproduction, and 'fastidious / difficult to feed' care difficulty notes.

additional.name: Tropical Fish Keeping
additional.notes: Used as a third confirming source for tank size (70 gal / ~265 L), semi-aggressive temperament, reef-safe-with-caution rating (clam-mantle and coral nipping reports), water parameters (22–26 °C, pH 8.1–8.4, SG 1.020–1.025, dKH 8–12), lifespan up to 8 years, sponge-inclusive diet requirement, and protogynous-hermaphrodite/haremic breeding biology.

fish.breedingNotes: Pelagic broadcast spawner. Captive rearing has reportedly been achieved at a commercial scale, and captive-bred specimens are now available through specialty aquaculture producers, though breeding at the hobbyist level is not routine. The species is generally considered a protogynous hermaphrodite — females can become male — and is believed to be haremic in the wild.
